ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Goan okra curry; mapped to the broader 'curry (food)' class FOODON_03311737 as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Goan okra curry; A0CKP (cooked vegetables, NFS) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Goan okra curry or sufficiently close analogue; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Goan Okra Curry weighs approximately 250 g, providing around 320 kcal, 25 g fat (18 g saturated), 20 g carbohydrate, and 6 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Okra / Bhindi curry with coconut milk; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for okra (ID 11278) and coconut milk (ID 12117) for micronutrient cross-check.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides the closest approved-source analogue for Indian vegetable curries with coconut milk. Macros and micronutrients are adapted from ICMR-NIN bhindi curry entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central okra (SR Legacy #11278) and coconut milk (SR Legacy #12117) component data. Input values (energy 128 kcal, protein 2.4 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 8.0 g, saturated fat 7.2 g, sodium 180 mg, fibre 2.8 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for coconut-milk-based vegetable curries and are retained. Cholesterol confirmed 0 mg (plant-based dish). Trans fat confirmed 0 g. Vitamin C 10 mg consistent with okra content per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable curry with coconut milk (GI ~35–45, low category).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Goan Okra Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the closest analogous entry: mixed vegetable curry with coconut milk (GI range 35–45). Okra's high soluble fibre (mucilage) content further attenuates glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 8.0 g carbohydrate × 2.5) / 100 = 8.0 g (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 180 mg/100 g; potassium ~220 mg/100 g; saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 180 mg/100 g is below the 200 mg threshold for hypertension concern; however, the high saturated fat from coconut milk (7.2 g/100 g) may contribute to endothelial dysfunction at large portions — limit serving to 150–200 g and avoid adding extra salt during cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~40 (low); dietary fibre 2.8 g/100 g; carbohydrate 8.0 g/100 g; okra soluble fibre (mucilage)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~40), moderate carbohydrate (8.0 g/100 g), and okra's soluble fibre (mucilage) collectively attenuate postprandial glucose rise; a 150 g portion delivers approximately 12 g carbohydrate and 4.2 g fibre, making this dish suitable for type 2 diabetes management when coconut milk quantity is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g from coconut milk; cholesterol 0 mg; trans fat 0 g; dietary fibre 2.8 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g (predominantly lauric acid from coconut milk) raises LDL-C concern for cardiovascular disease patients; limit portion to 150 g per meal and consider substituting light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat to approximately 3–4 g/100 g while retaining the fibre and anti-inflammatory spice benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g (pro-inflammatory at high intake); anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic); nightshade ingredient (red chili)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric curcumin, ginger, garlic) provide evidence-based benefit for RA; however, saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g from coconut milk may promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity — use light coconut milk and monitor for nightshade sensitivity from red chilies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goan Okra Curry is a traditional South Asian vegetable curry originating from Goa, India, and widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. It features ladies' fingers (okra / bhindi) simmered in a rich, spiced gravy made with full-fat coconut milk, dried red chilies, black pepper, tamarind, onion, garlic, ginger, turmeric, cumin, and coriander. The dish has a thick, slightly tangy, mildly spicy flavour profile with a silky texture from the coconut milk and a characteristic mucilaginous quality from the okra. It is fully plant-based, halal, vegan, vegetarian, and gluten-free. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 128 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 10.0 g fat (7.2 g saturated from coconut milk), 8.0 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g dietary fibre, and 180 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Okra's soluble fibre (mucilage) has evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties relevant to type 2 diabetes. The high saturated fat from coconut milk warrants portion control for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide additional clinical benefit. A standard serving is one bowl (250 g), delivering approximately 320 kcal and 8.0 g glycemic load.
common_user_queries: Is bhindi curry good for diabetes?
Goan curry with coconut milk nutrition
Is bhindi curry good for blood pressure?
Goan vegetable curry calories per bowl

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for coconut-milk-based Indian vegetable curries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data for okra and coconut milk. No exact dish entry exists in any approved source for 'Goan Okra Curry' specifically; values are adapted from the closest approved-source analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Goan okra curry with coconut milk is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific curry preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central composite entries. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient panel for this composite curry dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for okra and coconut milk. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based; no animal-derived cholesterol sources present. potassium_mg 220.0 mg/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central okra (SR Legacy #11278: 299 mg/100 g raw) and coconut milk (SR Legacy #12117: 263 mg/100 g) weighted by approximate recipe proportions; documented as an estimate. Input source listed as 'grabfood' with URL 'https://foodo.sg/' is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy and has been replaced with ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central as the approved nutrient evidence base. Primary cuisine field in input ('Curry Village-Authentic North Indian Cuisine - Boat Quay') is a restaurant name, not a cuisine classification; corrected to 'Indian — Goan'. country_of_origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'Goa, India (served in Singapore)' to reflect true culinary origin.
